title: ConfigureAwait(false) should be preferred
description: "ConfigureAwait(false)" should be usedAnalyze your codeintentionality-completemaintainabilityCode Smellmulti-threadingasync-awaitsuspiciousperformanceWhy is this an issue?After anawaitedTaskhas executed, you can continue execution in the original, calling thread or any arbitrary thread. Unless the rest of the code needs the context from which theTaskwas spawned,Task.ConfigureAwait(false)should be used to keep execution in theTaskthread to avoid the need for context switching and the possibility of deadlocks.This rule raises an issue when code in a class library targeting .Net Frameworkawaits aTaskand continues execution in the original calling thread.The rule does not raise for .Net Core libraries as there is noSynchronizationContextin .Net Core.Noncompliant code examplevar response = awai
recommendation: Refactor to eliminate the flagged pattern.

detection_conditions: Await call does not have .ConfigureAwait() appended
Code is in a library context (not UI code)
triggering_conditions: Any await expression lacking ConfigureAwait in library code
exclusion_conditions: UI code where synchronization context is needed
Explicitly documented reason for not using ConfigureAwait
checks.reasoning: In library code targeting .NET Framework, awaiting without ConfigureAwait(false) can cause deadlocks by capturing the synchronization context. The check_missing_configure_await function identifies await expressions lacking ConfigureAwait calls and reports them as violations. This prevents context switching overhead and potential deadlocks in library code while allowing explicit ConfigureAwait(true) when context is genuinely needed.

validation_examples.reason: Should use ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing context
Explicit ConfigureAwait(true) indicates intentional context capture
